But even if that were true the fact that they wanted the thing still shows the order was made to happen. So the person only got the order because of the feeling not because of the thing itself. So my answer is: The person who sell things should have used the order because it was made to cause the money back, which breaks the idea that the person likes always make money.

english_query: counterexample to: A theodicy T successfully reconciles the existence of a benevolent omnipotent deity with the existence of evil if and only if (i) T provides a morally sufficient reason for the deity's permission of evil, (ii) the reason cited in T is consistent with the deity's omnipotence and benevolence, and (iii) T accounts for the quantity and distribution of evil observed in the world.
ugf_response: First, we look at a way to pick the best path by looking at what happens after we make our choice. This way asks us to see if our act makes things better or worse for people who are there. If doing something makes more good than bad, it seems like a good thing to do. But then we must think about the other side. Some say a choice should be right even if no one gets hurt. For them, the good part is not enough to ignore if we are not kind. They might think that being mean is always bad, so we should be nice. This shows that just looking at what comes after can miss how we feel or if we want to help. If we only care about what is true now, we might do bad things because we cannot stop them from happening later. But if we want a world where everyone is safe and happy, we need to look at all the good together, not just the few bad parts. So, the idea that we only care if a choice is right is not strong because it ignores the big picture of how people live their lives. So my answer is: The idea that you must choose by what follows your acts is not strong. To find out if an act is really good, we need the full story of what comes next, not just what we wanted to happen at the start.
